story:
In fog so thick the stars are only a rumor, a tramp steamer inches toward harbor, its bell answering the buoy’s slow, double knock. Captain Mor’thn Weeds, oilskin collar up, watches dim lights swell from dimples to pinholes and counts time by the cowrie bracelet in his pocket. It is Christmas Eve. He carries a cargo of ermine pelts—one for each hard day at sea—and from the finest he has stitched a coat for the woman he calls his Bumblebee Lady. He aches for home and for the boy who must be five now, or six.

But the night carries a stain. Somewhere in the empty sea behind them, the Third Mate vanished. The man boarded with the crew and was entered on the departure manifest; he will not step ashore. What happened—fight, jealousy, grudge—no one says. On a tramp steamer, life can be raw, the code rough as a frontier town: swift retribution favored over patient justice. The crew has already acted, or allowed something to happen, and then turned their faces upward and away. They neither lied nor explained; they only shrugged. The empty bunk told Mor’thn what their silence would not. He knew enough to fear the truth, and not enough to name it.

Now the ship nears port, and Mor’thn must stand before the harbor Agent. If he reports a missing officer, there will be questions, delays, an inquiry dragged like a net through everyone’s lives. For whom would it be done—the lost man, his family, the Company, or simply because it is right? The crew wants only the quiet solution: alter the arrival report, close the book, say the Third Mate was never aboard when the ship made landfall. Once docked they will scatter to the winds, leaving no one eager to contradict him.

Mor’thn’s mind mutinies against that easy math. He knows too well how the law aboard an empty sea can harden into convenience. He hears an inner trinity—his God, his lover, himself—strain against a fourth, uninvited voice: conscience in the shape of the missing man. He weighs duty against love, obligation against homecoming. If he speaks, he risks angering the charterer, delaying his pay, postponing the embrace he has counted hours for. If he stays silent, he will bring his Bumblebee Lady the coat and the bright promise of Christmas—and carry a hidden tear in the net that may widen with time.

An hour earlier, while they still lay off the harbor waiting on the tide, Dankhov, his second mate, had looked up from their chessboard. What of the missing mate? The missing man has made a fourth voice of your conscience. Mor’thn had castled and said, “I guess he went for a swim and didn’t feel like coming back.” Dankhov had answered flatly: in that water, a man lasts minutes. Mor’thn had argued for nearly an hour, though he had not believed a word of it. Between the click of pieces and the roll of the ship, he had heard the bell and the buoy talk: Ka-long, Ka-lang. He had told himself he still had two more chances to change his mind before dawn, before the Agent asked his questions.

In the end, he knows he is responsible to the living and, if he is honest, to the lost. Bureaucracy is a tide, yes, but so is truth. On the gray edge of landfall, with Christmas lights wavering inside the fog, Mor’thn holds the choice that will define what kind of law he carried on the empty sea—and what kind of man will walk down the gangplank to those he loves.
